persons.arguments_against: The dossier warns of the single-initial namesake trap, and 'George Grey' is a genuinely common name (30 Greys in the staff lists, 12 in the biographies). But the merge does not rest on name or tenure overlap: (1) the Premiership of New Zealand is a unique office held by one person at a time, and the stint's 'Premier and Commissioner of Customs' records in 1878-79 fall exactly inside the biography's 'premier of New Zealand, 1877 to 1879'; (2) the stint's 1877 record is 'Superintendent of Auckland', matching the biography's 'superintendent of the Province of Auckland, 1875' — a second specific, unusual office in agreement; (3) every stint record carries K.C.B., and the biography's headline honour is K.C.B. (Civil.) 1848 — the deterministic honour check failed only on the '(Civil.)' string suffix. A namesake would have to be a second Sir George Grey, K.C.B., serving simultaneously as Superintendent of Auckland and Premier of New Zealand in the same years, which is impossible. No counter-hypothesis survives.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence that the New Zealand Premier of 1877-79 was a different George Grey from the former Governor, contradicting the biography's own statement of his premiership years.

notes: The honour check's FAIL is mechanical: bio 'K.C.B. (Civil.)' vs stint 'K.C.B.' is the same honour. Unique-office (Premier), exact-year, place, and honour agreement together make this certain despite the single-forename name.
